title: Offline Article Processing — When to Take an Article Offline
error_description: Article needs to be taken offline for manual processing. Conditions include: presence of landscape images, article opener page not properly rendered due to many authors or affiliations, print-based articles, force justify not yet implemented, short page not yet implemented, figure continued not yet implemented, presence of a greater number of figures and tables in the Appendix section with challenges in float ordering, complex decimal alignment in tables.
solution:
Take the article offline for manual intervention in these scenarios:
1. Landscape images — cannot be handled automatically.
2. Article opener page broken due to many authors or affiliations — needs manual layout.
3. Print-based articles.
4. Force justify, short page, or figure continued are not yet implemented in the tool.
5. Complex decimal alignment in tables.
6. Greater number of figures and tables in Appendix section where floats cannot be ordered sequentially.

Note: The following were previously offline scenarios but have been fixed:
- Inline tables and images (Fixed Apr 29)
- Long tables (Fixed Apr 29)
- Landscape tables (Fixed Apr 29)
- Endnotes (Fixed Sept 23)
- Straddle rule within tables (Fixed May 12)
- Footnote in First Page (Fixed Oct 2)

title: Known Pending Pain Points in LaTeX Proof Generation
error_description: Recurring unresolved issues in proof generation: orphan and widow lines, row-wise splitting of long tables, excess spacing (above headings, between floats and text, between equations), decimal alignment in tables, color figures converting to greyscale in printer files, equations not rendering correctly requiring manual LaTeX command adjustments.
solution:
1. Orphan/widow lines — addressed only via workarounds (adjusting image width, adding spacing above headings). Not consistently applied.
2. Row-wise splitting of long tables — not yet implemented. Results in excessive white space under continued tables.
3. Excess spacing — above headings, between floats and text, between equations — requires manual spacing controls.
4. Decimal alignment — unresolved; avoid "dot" alignment in table columns as a workaround.
5. Color figures to greyscale during printer export — requires offline intervention at the printer stage.
6. Equations not rendering as in editor — use \textrm or \mathrm instead of \rm.

title: Appendix Head Repeating — Duplicate Appendix Label in Heading
error_description: Duplicate Appendix labels appear in the heading after proof generation. For example, "Appendix A. Appendix A. Title" appears instead of "Appendix A. Title".
solution: The appendix label is duplicated because "Appendix A." is not tagged correctly as a label in the editor. Ensure the appendix label is properly tagged as a label element in appendix headings. Correct tagging of labels in appendix headings will prevent the duplication in the generated proof.

title: Author Queries — Only Author Queries Displayed in Proof
error_description: Publisher queries or Kriya automated queries are not appearing in the proof. Only author queries show up.
solution: The current proofing mechanism is configured to display only author queries. Publisher queries and Kriya automated queries are not included by design and will not appear in the proof. This is expected behaviour.

title: Query Page Not Rendering in Proof
error_description: The query page of an article is not rendering in the proof.
solution:
The query page renders only when the data-query-page="true" attribute is set on the title node of the article. Steps:
1. Click on the title paragraph.
2. Open the Insert menu.
3. Select Modify Attributes.
4. Click Add Attribute.
5. Enter data-query-page and set its value to true.

title: Author Query Numbers Skipped or Out of Sequence
error_description: Query numbers are being skipped in the proof — for example, the count jumps from AQ4 to AQ7. Some author queries are missing from the sequence.
solution: Skipped query numbers indicate that a query is placed inside a float-based environment such as a table. Place the query outside the float environment (outside the table block) to fix the sequence issue and make all queries appear correctly.

title: Equation Label or Number Missing in Proof
error_description: Equation label or equation number does not appear in the proof. The equation is inside a starred environment like \begin{align*} or \begin{equation*}.

title: Equations Right-Aligned Instead of Center-Aligned
error_description: Equations inside align or align* environment are right-aligned instead of center-aligned. No alignment marker is specified so LaTeX defaults to right alignment.
solution:
To center-align equations in the align environment, place the & symbol before the = sign in each equation. The & acts as an anchor point aligning all equations at the equal sign (equal aligning).

Example:
\begin{align}
    x + y & = z \\
    a + b & = c \\
    m - n & = p
\end{align}

title: Using \displaystyle at the Beginning of an Equation Has No Effect
error_description: The \displaystyle command is placed at the start of an equation environment (e.g., \displaystyle \begin{equation}...) but does not change the font or style of the equation as expected.

title: Display Equation Rendered as Inline Instead of Block
error_description: A display equation specified as block/display in Kriya is rendering as inline (on the same line as text) in the proof.
solution:
This is likely caused by using \begin{aligned}...\end{aligned} or \begin{gathered}...\end{gathered} environments. Replace them with their standalone alternatives:

- \begin{aligned}...\end{aligned} → \begin{align*}...\end{align*} (no equation number)
  Or \begin{align}...\end{align} (with equation number)

- \begin{gathered}...\end{gathered} → \begin{gather*}...\end{gather*}

- If it is a single equation, wrap the aligned environment inside an equation environment instead.

title: Using \rule Command in Equations Breaks Text Flow
error_description: The \rule command is used in inline or display equations. This breaks the flow of text in the PDF. \rule is not a recognized command in TeX for this purpose.
solution:
Replace \rule with \underline in all inline and display equations.

Incorrect: equation using \rule
Correct: replace with \underline

Using \underline achieves the same visual effect without breaking the text flow.

title: Equation Using {rc} Parameter with \begin{gather} Causes Rendering Error
error_description: An equation uses {rc} as a parameter with \begin{gather}{rc}...\end{gather}. This causes incorrect rendering in the proof.

title: Multiline Equation Using equation + split Causes Number Overlap
error_description: Multiline equations are written using \begin{equation}\begin{split}...\end{split}\end{equation}. The equation number overlaps with content, especially for long equations.

title: Reversible Arrow \xrightleftharpoons Not Rendering — Mathtools Package Missing
error_description: The \xrightleftharpoons command is not rendering or is not recognized. This command requires the mathtools package.
solution:
\xrightleftharpoons[below]{above} is provided by the mathtools package. If the package is not available, convert it to low-level commands:

\stackrel{above}{\underset{below}{symbol}}

Example conversion:
Original: A+B \xrightleftharpoons[k^{-}]{k^+} C
Converted: A + B \stackrel{k^+}{\underset{k^{-}}{\rightleftharpoons}} C

Structure:
- {k^+} goes above the arrow
- [k^-] goes below the arrow

title: Using \limits Inside \begin{cases} Causes Rendering Error
error_description: An equation uses \limits inside a \begin{cases} environment (e.g., \sum_\limits{...}). This causes incorrect rendering in the proof.

title: Algorithm Not Placed Correctly — Should Be Treated as Figure
error_description: An algorithm block is not being placed or formatted correctly in the proof. It needs to be treated as a figure/algorithm float.
solution:
To mark an element as an algorithm float:
1. Click on the figure/element you want to modify.
2. Open the Insert menu.
3. Select Modify Attributes.
4. Click Add Attribute.
5. Enter data-image-type and set its value to algorithm.

Note: If you cannot do this, contact support (L2) to add the attribute.

title: Float Placement Wrong — Figure or Table on Wrong Page
error_description: A figure or table is appearing on the wrong page or in the wrong order. Need to move a float to a different page or change the order of floats on a page.
solution:
Use the data-place-floats attribute on a paragraph to control float placement:
1. Click on the paragraph where you want the float to appear after.
2. Open the Insert menu → Select Modify Attributes → Click Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-place-floats and set its value to the float ID (e.g., BLK_F1).
4. For multiple floats, separate IDs with commas (e.g., BLK_F1,BLK_F2).

Important: This attribute works only on paragraphs — not on headings, equations, floats, or lists.

title: Figure Reordering Issue When Using data-place-floats Attribute
error_description: After applying data-place-floats to move Figure 2 to an earlier page, Figure 2 appears before Figure 1, causing figures to be out of sequence.
solution:
When multiple figures need to be placed together, always list ALL figures (including the ones already on the page) in the data-place-floats attribute in the correct order.

Incorrect (causes Figure 2 to appear before Figure 1):
data-place-floats="BLK_F2"

Correct (preserves sequence):
data-place-floats="BLK_F1,BLK_F2"

This ensures Figure 1 appears immediately after the paragraph, followed by Figure 2 in the correct order.

title: Placing a Figure Before a Table on the Same Page
error_description: On a page that has both a figure and a table, the table is appearing before the figure, but the figure should come first.
solution:
To place a figure before a table on the same page, go to the preceding page and add the data-place-floats attribute to the relevant paragraph with the table ID listed first, then the figure ID:

data-place-floats="BLK_table_ID,BLK_fig_ID"

Listing the table ID first ensures it is placed before the figure in the float queue, resulting in the figure appearing before the table on the output page.

title: Appendix Figure Appearing at Top of Page Instead of In-Place
error_description: An appendix figure is appearing at the top of the page by default (RS template places floats at the top), but it should appear exactly where it is referenced in the text (in-place).
solution:
Use data-floats-style="inplace" on the figure to override default top placement:
1. Click on the figure you want to modify.
2. Open the Insert menu → Select Modify Attributes → Click Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-floats-style and set its value to inplace.

Optionally, to add a barrier after the image (to control content flow before the image):
4. Enter data-floats-barrier and set its value to true.

Alternatively, tag the figure as inline to render it in-place.

Note: This attribute works only on figure floats, not on paragraphs, headings, equations, or lists.

title: Figure or Table Number Incorrect in Proof
error_description: The figure or table number in the proof is incorrect. For example, a correction article has only Figure 10, but the proof labels it Figure 1 because the template auto-counts from 1.
solution:
Override the auto-counter using the data-step-counter attribute:
1. Click on the figure or table that must be updated.
2. Open the Insert menu → Select Modify Attributes → Click Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-step-counter and set its value to the correct number (e.g., 12 for Figure 12).

Example: data-step-counter = "12"

Note for equations: Do not enter a decimal label (e.g., 3.5). Enter the ordinal position of the equation (e.g., 5 for the 5th equation).

title: Content Overflows Page — Need to Push Content to Next Page After Float
error_description: Content after a figure or table overflows the page due to tight float placement. Need to force content after the float to move to the next page.
solution:
Use the data-clearpage="true" attribute on the figure or table to push content after it to the next page:
1. Click on the figure or table.
2. Open the Insert menu → Select Modify Attributes → Click Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-clearpage and set its value to true.

Note: This attribute works only on figure/table floats, not on paragraphs, headings, equations, or lists.

title: Adding Space Above or Below a Paragraph
error_description: Need to add space above or below a specific paragraph in the proof to improve layout.
solution:
Use data-space-top and data-space-bottom attributes on the paragraph:
1. Click on the paragraph.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-space-top with a numeric value to add lines above (e.g., data-space-top="1" adds one line above).
4. Enter data-space-bottom with a numeric value to add lines below (e.g., data-space-bottom="1" adds one line below).

Note: These attributes work only on paragraphs — not on headings, equations, floats, or lists.

title: Adding or Removing Space Above a Heading
error_description: Too much or too little space above a heading in the proof. Need to adjust spacing above a heading.
solution:
Use the data-top-gap attribute on the heading:
1. Click on the heading.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-top-gap with a point value.

To add space: data-top-gap="12pt" (adds 12pt above the heading)
To remove space: data-top-gap="-12pt" (reduces space above heading by 12pt)

Note: This attribute works only on headings — not on paragraphs, equations, floats, or lists.

title: Gaining or Losing a Line in a Paragraph or List Item
error_description: A paragraph or list item needs to be stretched to gain a line (add a line) or tightened to lose a line to fix layout issues like widow/orphan lines.
solution:
Use data-gain-a-line or data-loose-a-line attributes:
1. Click on the paragraph or list item.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. To gain a line: Enter data-gain-a-line with value "true".
4. To tighten/lose a line: Enter data-loose-a-line with value "true".

Note: These attributes work only on paragraphs — not on headings, equations, floats, or lists.

title: Indenting or Removing Indent from a Paragraph
error_description: A paragraph is incorrectly indented or needs indentation removed in the proof.
solution:
To indent/remove indent via proof controls:
- Select the paragraph → Choose proof controls → Select "remove indent paragraph" option → Click Add or Remove.

To prevent indentation using attributes:
1. Select the paragraph.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Add data-indent and set its value to an integer (e.g., 12).

Note: The numerical value is relevant for AHS proofing but does not affect LaTeX typesetting. LaTeXSetter applies indentation based on template configuration and ignores this numerical value.

This attribute works only on paragraphs — not on headings, equations, floats, or lists.

title: Table Too Wide — Does Not Fit on Page
error_description: A table is too wide to fit on the page. The content overflows or is cut off.
solution:
Reduce the font size of the table to make it fit:
1. Adjust the font size of the table using table setter to a lower value.
2. Generate the proof again after reducing the font size.

This is the recommended approach when the table must remain on a single page and cannot be reformatted otherwise.

title: Table Column Decimal Alignment Breaking Proof or PDF Half-Generated
error_description: Table alignment is breaking and text like "[table-column-width=..." appears in the proof. Or the PDF is generated only halfway. One or more columns have decimal (dot) alignment.
solution:
1. Preview the table using table setter.
2. Check the alignment of each column.
3. If any column alignment is set to "dot", change it to "None".
4. Re-proof the manuscript.

Important: If you change the table layout from "single" to "double" or "landscape", the column alignment will automatically reset. After any layout change, recheck all column alignments and reset any that reverted to "dot".

title: Removing Column Separation Space in Tables
error_description: Table columns have too much space between them (default is 6pt in LaTeX templates). Need to remove or reduce column separation.
solution:
Use the data-remove-colsep attribute on the table:
1. Select the table.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-remove-colsep and set its value to "true".

This removes the default 6pt column separation space.

Note: This attribute works only on tables — not on headings, equations, floats, or list items.

title: Deprecated LaTeX Formatting Commands — \bf \it \rm \sf \tt
error_description: Old-style LaTeX commands such as \bf, \it, \rm, \sf, or \tt are used for text formatting. These are deprecated and do not work well with modern font encodings.

title: Bold Calligraphic Math Symbols — \boldsymbol{\mathcal{}} Not Supported
error_description: Equations use $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{E}}$ (or similar bold calligraphic symbols). The \boldsymbol command is not supported in the LaTeX template and does not render correctly.
solution:
Replace \boldsymbol{\mathcal{}} with \mathbfcal{}:

Incorrect: $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{E}}$
Correct: $\mathbfcal{E}$

Apply this replacement to all instances of \boldsymbol{\mathcal{...}} in the document.

title: Greek Symbols Rendering Italic Instead of Upright (Royal Society Journals)
error_description: In Royal Society journals, uppercase Greek symbols such as \Gamma, \Delta, \Theta, \Lambda, \Xi, \Pi, \Sigma, \Upsilon, \Phi, \Psi, \Omega are rendering in italic style instead of upright (roman) style, even though MathJax renders them upright.
solution:
This is by design for Royal Society journals. The Royal Society template redeclares these symbols to their italic variants.

These standard commands render italic in the RS template:
\Gamma, \Delta, \Theta, \Lambda, \Xi, \Pi, \Sigma, \Upsilon, \Phi, \Psi, \Omega

If upright rendering is required, use the corresponding \var versions:
Example: \Delta → \varDelta

MathJax renders these symbols upright by default, but the Royal Society LaTeX template overrides this to use italic style.

title: Proof Generation Failed — Unsupported Equation Commands
error_description: Proof generation fails completely. No proof is produced. Multiple unsupported equation commands are present in the editor.
solution:
Proof generation will fail if the document contains unsupported equation commands. Steps to resolve:
1. Check the editor for unsupported LaTeX equation commands.
2. If there are multiple occurrences of unsupported equations, the proof generation will fail.
3. Identify the unsupported commands and replace them with supported equivalents.
4. Common unsupported commands: \rule in equations (replace with \underline), \boldsymbol{\mathcal{}} (replace with \mathbfcal{}), physics package commands like \qty or \grad.
5. After replacing all unsupported commands, regenerate the proof.

title: Unwanted Content Moving to First Page Gap (Royal Society) — data-skip-fpsplit
error_description: A paragraph or heading is incorrectly moving to fill the gap between the abstract block and the copyright statement block on the first page of a Royal Society journal article, causing layout issues.
solution:
Use the data-skip-fpsplit="true" attribute to prevent the node from moving to the first page:
1. Click on the paragraph or heading that should not move to the first page.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-skip-fpsplit and set its value to "true".

Note: Adding data-skip-fpsplit to a node will also prevent all subsequent consecutive nodes from moving to the first page.

title: Abstract Split Causing Widow/Orphan Line on Second Page (Royal Society) — data-split-abs
error_description: The abstract paragraph splits on the first page of a Royal Society article, but the split point results in a widow or orphan line on the second page.
solution:
Use the data-split-abs="true" attribute on an empty span node inserted at the desired split point in the abstract:

1. Identify the exact word in the abstract where the split should occur to avoid the widow/orphan line.
2. Insert an empty span node at that position.
3. Add the attribute data-split-abs="true" to that span node.

Important: There is currently no proof control available in the editor for this. This must be done by the support team (L2). Raise a request if this adjustment is needed.

title: Left Panel Spacing on First Page Too Large (Royal Society) — data-adjust-stub-space
error_description: The left panel (stub area) spacing on the first page of a Royal Society journal article is too large and needs to be reduced.
solution:
Use the data-adjust-stub-space attribute on the journal title:
1. Click on the title of the journal.
2. Open Insert → Modify Attributes → Add Attribute.
3. Enter data-adjust-stub-space and set its value to the number of points to reduce (e.g., data-adjust-stub-space="1").

Note: The value reduces the spacing in points (pt).

title: Folio Block or Page Number Missing from Proof
error_description: The folio block or page number is missing from a page in the proof. The page with the missing folio contains an image.
solution: If the folio block or page number is missing on a page that contains an image, try reducing the width of the image on that page. Reducing the image width often resolves folio block rendering issues caused by the image taking up too much space on the page.

title: Physics Package Commands Not Rendering — \qty \grad Not Recognized (Royal Society)
error_description: Commands from the physics package such as \qty or \grad are not rendering correctly or are unrecognized in the proof. The Royal Society LaTeX template does not include the physics package.
solution:
The physics package is not supported in the Royal Society LaTeX template. Commands like \qty and \grad will not be recognized and will cause incorrect rendering of equations or text.

The physics package cannot be imported globally because it redeclares some standard commands like \div, which would break other functionality.

Resolution: Replace all physics package commands with standard LaTeX equivalents:
- \qty{value}{unit} → write out the value and unit manually
- \grad → \nabla or write gradient explicitly

Refer to the standard Royal Society template for supported commands.

title: Why tabularray Package Is Used Instead of tabular
error_description: Questions about why tabularray is used instead of the standard tabular package. Or tabular-specific formatting is not working as expected.
solution:
The tabularray package is used instead of tabular for these reasons:

1. Row spanning and line rules: In tabular, horizontal lines (rules) often appear within spanned rows, disrupting visual structure. tabularray ensures horizontal lines appear only at the end of a row, keeping formatting clean.

2. Inline long tables: Inline long tables are not supported in the tabular environment. tabularray handles them seamlessly.

If you are experiencing issues with tabular-specific commands, they may need to be converted to tabularray syntax.

title: Applying Force Justification to a Paragraph
error_description: A paragraph needs force justification — the text needs to break at a specific word and the line should be force-justified.
solution:
To force justify content within a paragraph:
1. Select the Breakpoint — highlight the word in the paragraph where you want the content to break and move to the next line.
2. Hover over the selected text to reveal Proof Control.
3. Click on Proof Control.
4. Click the dropdown and select Force Justify.

This breaks the content at the selected word and applies force justification within the proof.

Note: Force justify is not yet fully implemented as a general feature — use this proof control method as the available workaround.

title: What is Kriyadocs?
error_description: User asks what Kriyadocs is, what the company does, or what platform they are using.
solution:
Kriyadocs is an end-to-end scholarly publishing platform that enables authors, editors, copy-editors, proof-readers, typesetters, and all key stakeholders to collaborate and produce high-quality content faster.

Kriyadocs is built and operated by Exeter Premedia Services Private Limited, headquartered in Chennai, India.

The platform covers the full publishing lifecycle — from editorial workflows and peer review to typesetting, XML conversion, and final output generation (print PDF, online PDF, XML, HTML, EPUB, and more).

Kriyadocs serves academic, professional, and scholarly publishers globally and has 30,000+ users worldwide.

title: Kriyadocs Company History and Background
error_description: User asks about Kriyadocs company history, founding year, parent company, location, or experience.
solution:
Kriyadocs is a product of Exeter Premedia Services Private Limited.

- Founded: Exeter Premedia Services was established in 2004.
- Kriyadocs platform launched: 2014.
- Headquarters: Chennai, India.
- Users: 30,000+ globally.
- Certifications: ISO 9001:2015 (quality management) and ISO 27001:2013 (information security).

With 20+ years of experience in the publishing industry, the company combines domain expertise with modern technology to deliver a reliable and innovative publishing ecosystem.

title: what is peer Review?
error_description: what is peer Review?
solution: Don't worry if you're unsure - Peer Review is essentially when experts in the same field review and give feedback on your work, usually to help improve its quality or accuracy before it's published. It's like getting a second set of eyes to check over your writing, editing, and formatting for that extra special touch!

title: What is DTD validation?
error_description: What is DTD validation?
solution: I sense a curious mind! DTD (Document Type Definition) validation is basically a way to check if an HTML or XML document adheres to specific rules and structure guidelines, ensuring it's well-formed and looks its best. Think of it like proofreading your manuscript before publishing – it helps catch errors and makes sure everything is in order!

title: BibTeX citation style for LaTeX document
error_description: BibTeX citation style for LaTeX document
solution:
Citations and referencing can sometimes be a bit tricky, especially when switching between different bibliography styles or tools.

To make it simple: **In citations, the goal is to acknowledge the original source of an idea, quote, or piece of information**. You're essentially saying, "Hey, I got this from someone/something else; here's where they got it from."

Think of references like a breadcrumb trail that helps you (and others) understand where your ideas come from and how they relate to the bigger picture.

There are different types of citations and referencing styles, such as:

* **Author-Date style** (e.g., APA): uses a specific format for citing sources, usually with author's name, year of publication, and page numbers.
* **Note-Bibliography style** (e.g., MLA): includes notes at the end of the document with citations, followed by a separate bibliography list.

When switching between different citation styles or tools, you might need to adapt your references to fit the new format. This can be a bit tedious, but it's an important part of maintaining academic integrity and following guidelines set by your institution or publication.

Does that make sense?

title: Explain about references citation.
error_description: Explain about references citation.
solution:
Let's dive into reference citations.

**What are reference citations?**

Reference citations, also known as bibliographic citations or author-date citations, are used to acknowledge the sources of information in academic and research writing. They provide a way to credit the original authors of ideas, data, and research findings.

**Why do we use reference citations?**

Using reference citations is essential for:

1. **Avoiding plagiarism**: By citing sources accurately, you avoid passing off someone else's work as your own.
2. **Giving credit**: References help acknowledge the contributions of other researchers, scholars, or authors who have helped shape our understanding of a topic.
3. **Building credibility**: Properly cited references demonstrate your commitment to academic integrity and your ability to evaluate sources critically.

**Types of reference citations**

There are several citation styles, including:

1. **Author-Date System** (e.g., APA, MLA): This system uses parentheses with the author's last name and publication year.
2. **Numerical Citing** (e.g., Chicago): This system uses numbers to cite sources in the text.
3. **Harvard Style**: This system uses a superscript number for citations.

**Common reference citation formats**

Here are some examples of common references:

* Book: Lastname, Firstname. Title of book. Publisher, Year.
* Journal Article: Author's Lastname, F. Firstname L. "Article title." Journal name Volume(Issue): pp-pp. Year.
* Online Source: Author's Lastname, F. Firstname L. "Article title." Website Title, Day Month Year, URL. Accessed Date.

**Best practices**

When using reference citations:

1. **Consistency is key**: Stick to a consistent citation style throughout your work.
2. **Check for accuracy**: Verify the accuracy of your references by cross-checking them with the original sources.
3. **Keep it concise**: Use concise citations that don't disrupt the flow of your writing.

Give these a try! If the issue still persists after checking these, please contact our support team — they'll look into it personally.

title: BibTeX manuscript submission peer review process
error_description: BibTeX manuscript submission peer review process
solution:
**Peer Review**

Peer Review is the process where experts in the same field evaluate and provide feedback on a manuscript or submission to ensure its quality, validity, and relevance. This helps maintain high standards of research and publishing accuracy. 

Here are some checkpoints to try:

*   Check for grammar, punctuation, and spelling errors
*   Verify formatting consistency throughout the document
*   Ensure proper citations and references

Give these a try! If the issue still persists after checking these, please contact our support team — they'll look into it personally.

title: Give me some examples in reference citations.
error_description: Give me some examples in reference citations.
solution:
**Examples of Reference Citations**

Here are a few examples of common types of reference citations:

*   **Author-Date Style**
    *   Example: (Smith, 2020)
    *   Explanation: This style is commonly used in fields like science and social sciences.
*   **MLA Style**
    *   Example: (Lastname Firstname) 24
    *   Explanation: MLA style is often used in humanities and liberal arts disciplines.

**More Examples**

Here are a few more examples of common reference citations:

*   **APA Style**
    *   Example: Smith, J. D., & Johnson, K. T. (2020). The effects of climate change on global food systems.
    *   Explanation: APA style is widely used in social sciences and educational fields.

*   **Chicago Style**

    *   Example: John Smith, The History of Science (New York: Random House, 2019), 123-145.
    *   Explanation: This style is commonly used in history and humanities.

**Remember to always check the specific citation style required by your instructor or publication.

title: What is xml
error_description: XML validation error publishing
solution:
I'm happy to help you understand more about XML!

**What is XML?**

XML stands for eXtensible Markup Language. It's a markup language that's used to store and transport data between systems, applications, or organizations. Think of it like a set of rules that helps different systems "speak" the same language.

**Key characteristics of XML:**

* **Platform-independent**: XML can be easily read by humans and machines on any device or operating system.
* **Self-descriptive**: Each element in an XML document contains metadata, which explains its purpose and structure.
* **Hierarchical structure**: XML documents consist of a tree-like structure with elements and attributes that are nested inside each other.

**How is XML used?**

XML is commonly used for:

* **Data exchange**: Sharing data between different systems or applications using a standard format.
* **Configuration files**: Storing configuration settings, such as user preferences or application settings.
* **Web services**: Sending messages between web services and applications.
* **Document storage**: Saving and retrieving documents in a structured format.

**What does XML look like?**

XML documents typically consist of:

* **Elements**, which are represented by tags (`<` and `>`) with a name (e.g., `<book>`).
* **Attributes**, which provide additional information about an element (e.g., `<book author="John">`).
* **Text content**, which is the actual data stored in the document.

For example:
```xml
<book>
  <title>The Great Gatsby</title>
  <author>John</author>
  <year>1925</year>
</book>
```
This XML snippet represents a book with three elements: title, author, and year.

title: LaTeX spacing around paragraphs proof pages
error_description: LaTeX spacing around paragraphs proof pages
solution:
**Adjusting Space Before and After Paragraphs in Proofs**

Here are some steps to help you achieve this:

1. **Use the `before` and `after` options**: Within your proof environment, use `\vspace{<length>}` to add space before a paragraph, and `\quad` or `\hspace{<length>}` to add space after.
2. **Use the `flushrule` option**: This can help balance spacing on both sides of a paragraph.
3. **Check your LaTeX document class**: Different classes might have different settings for these options.

Please try these steps:

1. Open your LaTeX file and look for sections where you want to adjust the space around paragraphs.
2. Use `\vspace{<length>}` before the paragraph, e.g., `\vspace{10pt}`.
3. Use `\quad` or `\hspace{<length>}` after the paragraph, e.g., `\quad` after the text.

Give these a try! If you still have issues, please contact our support team — they'll look into it personally.

title: DTD Error
error_description: DTD Error
solution:
**What is DTD?**
```xml
<!DOCTYPE article-system PUBLIC "-//OASIS//DTD DocBook XML v1.0//EN" "http://www.oasis-open.org/docbook/01-05.dtd">
```
A **DTD (Document Type Definition)** is a set of rules that defines the structure and organization of an XML document. It's like a blueprint for your manuscript, ensuring it adheres to specific guidelines.

**What causes a DTD Error?**
```xml
Error: DTD validation failed on line 123.
```
A **DTD error** occurs when an element or attribute doesn't match the defined rules in the DTD. This can be due to incorrect markup, missing or extra tags, or other inconsistencies.

**How to fix a DTD Error?**
```markdown
1. Check your XML document for any errors or inconsistencies.
2. Review the relevant DTD definition to understand what's required.
3. Make necessary corrections and re-validate your document using the DTD.
```
For more detailed troubleshooting, please consult the specific DTD being used (e.g., Journal Article Tag Suite).

title: Incomplete PDF Proof Output
error_description: During proof generation, the proof was not generated for the entire article. Only the first half of the article was rendered, and the remaining content did not appear in the proof.
solution:
1. Check whether any DTD, Probe, or PMC errors have been thrown.
2. Verify whether the export XML has been generated successfully.
3. Check all table cells for "DOT" alignment. If present, change the alignment to "None."
4. Open and validate all display equations.
5. Verify that all figures are loaded correctly in their respective figure blocks.
6. Analyze all references for invalid links, extra spaces, or formatting issues. Validate each reference and ensure that all links are working correctly.

title: Kriyadocs Services and Offerings
error_description: User asks what services Kriyadocs provides, what the platform can do, or what features are available.
solution:
Kriyadocs provides an end-to-end publishing services ecosystem, including:

1. Editorial workflow management — peer review, submissions, and editorial tracking.
2. Typesetting — LaTeX, InDesign, 3B2, QuarkXPress, XSL-FO.
3. XML/HTML conversion — structured content output for online publishing.
4. EPUB/Ebook generation — EPUB, Mobipocket, KF8, Nook formats.
5. Copyediting and proofreading — professional language services.
6. Metadata enrichment — DOI, Crossref, PubMed, indexing.
7. PDF output — print-ready and online PDFs.
8. Document collaboration — cloud-based real-time editing and review.
9. Project management and author liaison.

The platform supports journals, books, and conference proceedings.

title: What is KriyaIQ?
error_description: User asks what KriyaIQ is, who built KriyaIQ, or what KriyaIQ can help with.
solution:
KriyaIQ is the AI-powered support assistant built by Kriyadocs.

KriyaIQ is designed to help typesetters, editors, and platform users quickly resolve technical issues and questions related to the Kriyadocs platform and its publishing workflows.

KriyaIQ can help with:
- LaTeX typesetting errors and formatting issues
- XML, HTML, EPUB, and PDF publishing problems
- Metadata, validation, and Crossref queries
- InDesign, Calibre, and rendering issues
- General e-publishing workflow questions

For complex or unresolved issues, KriyaIQ will connect you to the human support team.

title: Kriyadocs Platform Features for Journals and Books
error_description: User asks about what the Kriyadocs platform offers for journal publishing or book publishing.
solution:
The Kriyadocs platform is built for both journal and book publishers:

For Journals:
- Peer review and submission management
- Editorial workflow automation
- Author and reviewer collaboration tools
- XML-first typesetting pipeline
- DOI registration and Crossref integration
- PubMed, Scopus, and indexing support

For Books:
- Multi-chapter manuscript management
- InDesign and LaTeX typesetting
- EPUB and ebook generation
- Index and TOC automation
- Print and digital PDF outputs

The platform is cloud-based and enables all stakeholders to collaborate in real time.

title: Kriyadocs Quality Standards and Certifications
error_description: User asks about Kriyadocs quality standards, certifications, or compliance.
solution:
Kriyadocs / Exeter Premedia Services is a process-driven, ISO-certified organization:

- ISO 9001:2015 — Quality Management System certification, ensuring consistent delivery of high-quality publishing services.
- ISO 27001:2013 — Information Security Management System certification, ensuring client data and manuscripts are handled securely.

The company's core philosophy is to provide an exceptional experience to authors, resulting in superior outcomes for publishers and all stakeholders in the publishing ecosystem.

title: Kriyadocs Typesetting Technologies Supported
error_description: User asks what typesetting software or technologies Kriyadocs supports.
solution:
Kriyadocs supports a wide range of typesetting and composition technologies:

- LaTeX — Full support for scientific and mathematical typesetting.
- InDesign — Used for complex layouts and high-design publications.
- 3B2 (Advent 3B2) — Legacy typesetting for established publishers.
- QuarkXPress — Supported for publishers using this composition system.
- XSL-FO — For XML-driven typesetting pipelines.

The Data Services team is well-versed in all these technologies and can handle complex equations, tables, figures, and multilingual content across all formats.

title: How to Contact Kriyadocs Support
error_description: User asks how to contact Kriyadocs, reach customer support, or escalate an issue.
solution:
To reach Kriyadocs support:

1. Use KriyaIQ (this assistant) for immediate help with common issues.
2. For unresolved or complex issues, contact the Kriyadocs support team directly through your publisher portal or by emailing the support address provided by your publisher account manager.
3. Visit kriyadocs.com for platform information and resources.

When contacting support, please include:
- Your publisher/journal name
- Article or manuscript ID (if applicable)
- Description of the issue and steps already tried
- Any error messages or screenshots

The support team is responsive and will personally look into your issue.

solution: 1. Log into Kriyadocs using the unique link provided. 2. Alternatively, access the article from the history tab. 3. For publishers, log into the Kriyadocs dashboard with email and password to access articles.

Use the profile picture at the top right corner to access options such as changing password, checking email notifications, and logging out.
